title: 1.2 Arrays & Strings
summary: List operations and their complexities. String immutability gotcha. Traversal patterns. Hashmap intro via is_anagram.
sessionNotes: Good instincts on data structure choice; needed reminders on Python syntax; caught bugs with guidance.
intro: List access is O(1), insert at front is O(n) (shifts everything). Strings are immutable — `+=` in a loop is O(n²). Build with a list + `"".join()` instead. Prefer `enumerate` over `range(len(...))`. The anagram problem introduces dict-as-counter — key is the character, value is how many times it appeared.

body.heading: List operations and their complexities
body.content: Index access is O(1). Append to the end is O(1) amortized. Insert at the FRONT is O(n) — every other element has to shift right by one.

body.heading: List ops — fast vs slow
body.content:
lst = [10, 20, 30]

# O(1) — direct index
print(lst[1])

# O(1) amortized — append to end
lst.append(40)
print(lst)

# O(n) — insert at front shifts everything right
lst.insert(0, 0)
print(lst)

body.heading: String immutability — the gotcha
body.content:
Strings are IMMUTABLE in Python. Every `+=` builds a NEW string by copying the previous one. Across n iterations, that's 1+2+...+n = O(n²) work.

The canonical fix: collect parts in a list, then `"".join()` at the end → O(n) total.

body.heading: BAD: O(n²) via string +=
GOOD: O(n) via join
Traversal patterns
body.content:
Three common shapes:
  1. Values only: `for item in lst:`
  2. Index + value: `for i, item in enumerate(lst):` — preferred over `range(len(...))`
  3. Reverse: `for item in reversed(lst):` — or `range(len(s)-1, -1, -1)` if you need the index too

exercises.title: Exercise 1 — find_index
exercises.prompt: Write find_index(lst, target): return the index of the first occurrence of target, or -1 if not found. Use enumerate.
exercises.critique: Clean. Correctly uses enumerate, returns -1 on miss. Standard linear search.

exercises.title: Exercise 2 — reverse_string (no built-ins)
exercises.prompt: Write reverse_string(s): take a string and return it reversed. Do NOT use Python's built-in reversed() or [::-1]. Think about what data structure to build your result in.
exercises.critique: Correct and idiomatic. Builds with a list to avoid the string-immutability O(n²) trap, joins at the end. The `range(len(s)-1, -1, -1)` is the canonical reverse-index pattern — also the one that keeps resetting in the review queue.

exercises.title: Exercise 3 — is_anagram
exercises.prompt: Write is_anagram(s1, s2): True if s2 is an anagram of s1. Same characters, same counts, different order.
exercises.critique: Works correctly. Good instinct: short-circuit on length mismatch. Two-pass: build counts from s1, decrement on s2. The `-1` check catches the case where s2 has more of a letter than s1 — though once length is equal AND no missing letters, you don't strictly need it. Refinement: `d[k] = d.get(k, 0) + 1` would replace the if/else block in one line — that's the counting idiom that keeps coming up.

reviewItemsAdded: String immutability — `+=` in loop is O(n²)
Dict syntax: `{}`, `d[key] = val`, `if key in d`
Dict as hashmap — choosing what the value represents
